skill.name: python-performance-optimization
skill.description: Profile and optimize Python code with CPU profiling, memory profiling, benchmarking, and practical performance patterns.
skill.summary: A Python performance guide for profiling bottlenecks, reducing memory use, and improving runtime behavior.
skill.author: ActiveInferenceInstitute

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, SQLite, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance patterns. Review found these are Markdown examples in a Python performance guide, with no prompt injection, obfuscation, credential access, or automatic execution. The remaining risk is low because users may copy examples that run shell commands, make demo HTTP requests, inspect local processes, or create local files.

low_findings.title: External Command Alerts Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The external command findings map to Markdown code fences and copy-paste profiling examples. They are not executed by the skill, but users should review commands before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a Markdown guide with fenced Python and Bash examples. No scripts, hooks, or automation files execute these commands automatically.

low_findings.title: Demo Network Requests in Async I/O Example
low_findings.description: The network findings are example requests to httpbin.org for benchmarking synchronous and asynchronous HTTP behavior. No secrets or local data are sent in the example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code uses requests and aiohttp with hardcoded httpbin delay URLs. This is outbound network activity if copied, but the context is an educational benchmark.

low_findings.title: Local File and SQLite Examples
low_findings.description: The guide shows writing a profiler output file, opening a caller-provided filename, and connecting to example.db. These are local filesystem examples, not hidden data collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are visible tutorial snippets with fixed or caller-supplied local paths. I found no evidence of secret file targeting or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Process Profiling Requires Authorization
low_findings.description: The py-spy examples inspect a running process by PID and can reveal stack traces. This is legitimate profiler usage, but users should only run it on authorized processes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are standard py-spy profiling commands shown in a production profiling section. They can expose process internals, but there is no malicious target or automation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings appear to match profiling-related text, not cryptographic APIs. No MD5, SHA-1, DES, or similar weak algorithm usage was found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain the skill description and an @profile decorator example. They do not contain cryptographic functions or hash algorithms.

low_findings.title: Critical Heuristic Dismissed After Context Review
low_findings.description: The critical combination appears to combine unrelated tutorial snippets for code examples, HTTP benchmarking, and SQLite use. No evidence found of credential access, obfuscation, or exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no credential reads, encoded payloads, suspicious domains, or instructions to bypass security analysis. The risky patterns are isolated educational examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copy-Paste Shell Profiling Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes Bash commands for cProfile, pstats, py-spy, and pytest-benchmark. These are expected for the topic but should be run only against trusted local projects and processes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly visible in fenced or commented examples. They are not hidden execution paths, but they can affect local profiling output or inspect process state.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound HTTP Benchmark Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The async I/O section includes hardcoded httpbin.org URLs and HTTP client libraries. Running the snippet will contact an external service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded URLs and requests/aiohttp usage are direct evidence of network behavior if copied. The destination is a common public test endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Profiling and Database Artifacts
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples write or open local files, including profile_output.prof and example.db. These examples are transparent and limited to local paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file paths are shown directly in tutorial code. I found no attempt to access credential files or transmit file contents.

content.value_statement: Slow Python code wastes engineering time and can hide the real bottleneck. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through profiling, benchmarking, memory checks, and focused optimization patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains CPU profiling with cProfile, pstats, line_profiler, and py-spy.
Shows memory profiling methods with memory_profiler, tracemalloc, generators, iterators, and weak references.
Compares common implementation patterns such as list comprehensions, joins, dictionaries, caching, and NumPy vectorization.
Provides examples for multiprocessing, async I/O, database batching, indexing, and query planning.
Includes benchmark patterns using timeit, decorators, and pytest-benchmark.
Supplies a checklist for profiling, optimizing, and validating performance changes.
limitations: The skill is a guide and does not run profiling tools by itself.
Performance advice must be validated with measurements from the target application.
Some examples require optional packages such as line-profiler, memory-profiler, py-spy, NumPy, aiohttp, or pytest-benchmark.
Production profiling and HTTP examples need user authorization and environment review before execution.

use_cases.title: Find Slow Python Functions
use_cases.description: Use profiling output to identify functions with high cumulative time and prioritize fixes.

use_cases.title: Reduce Memory Pressure
use_cases.description: Compare list, generator, iterator, and cache patterns to reduce unnecessary allocation.

use_cases.title: Validate Optimization Work
use_cases.description: Create repeatable benchmarks before and after code changes to confirm real improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Profile a Slow Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to help me profile a slow Python script. Start with safe local steps and explain what each profiler result means.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Memory Usage
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Python workflow for memory pressure. Suggest generator, iterator, tracemalloc, or cache changes and explain how to measure impact.

prompt_templates.title: Benchmark Competing Implementations
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare these Python implementations with a repeatable benchmark plan. Include metrics, warmup concerns, and how to avoid misleading results.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a Production Hot Path
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a production-safe performance investigation for this hot path. Include profiling tools, risks, rollback criteria, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: A developer reports that a request handler became slow after a data-processing change.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes a profiling plan, identifies likely hot functions from cumulative timing, and recommends targeted benchmark checks before changing code.

output_examples.input: A data job runs out of memory when processing a large input file.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains how to compare list loading with iterator processing, track allocations, and confirm lower peak memory usage.

output_examples.input: A team wants to speed up repeated database inserts.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends batching, fewer commits, query planning, and before-and-after measurements tied to the current database workload.

best_practices: Measure first, then optimize the functions or queries that dominate runtime.
Use representative input data and compare results before and after each change.
Review shell, network, and production profiling examples before running them in sensitive environments.
anti_patterns: Do not optimize code paths without profiling evidence.
Do not treat microbenchmarks as proof of production performance without workload validation.
Do not run process profiling or network examples on systems where you lack authorization.

faq.question: Does this skill execute profiling tools automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples that a user can review and run manually.

faq.question: Which profilers does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers cProfile, pstats, line_profiler, memory_profiler, py-spy, tracemalloc, and pytest-benchmark.

faq.question: Can it help with memory leaks?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains allocation snapshots, garbage collection checks, iterators, generators, and weak-reference cache patterns.

faq.question: Does it include database performance advice?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers batching writes, using indexes, query planning, and selecting only needed columns.

faq.question: Are there security concerns?
faq.answer: The main concern is copy-paste execution of examples that run commands, inspect processes, access local files, or make demo HTTP requests.

faq.question: Is it useful with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance can help these tools reason about Python profiling plans, benchmarks, and optimization tradeoffs.
